introduction.p: We understand the theoretical rule behind the function f(t) = \sin(t): given an angle t in radians, \sin(t) measures the value of the y-coordinate of the corresponding point on the unit circle. For special values of t, we have determined the exact value of \sin(t). For example, \sin(\frac{\pi}{3}) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}. But note that we don't have a formula for \sin(t). Instead, we use a button on our calculator or command on our computer to find values like \sin(1.35). It turns out that a combination of calculus and polynomial functions explains how computers determine values of the sine function.

At http://gvsu.edu/s/0zA, you'll find a Desmos worksheet that has the sine function already defined, along with a sequence of polynomials labeled T_1(x), T_3(x), T_5(x), T_7(x), \ldots. You can see these functions' graphs by clicking on their respective icons.

statement.p: For what values of x does it appear that \sin(x) \approx T_1(x)?

solution.p: The polynomial T_1(x) = x. It appears that \sin(x) \approx T_1(x) for values of x near 0, roughly for |x| \lesssim 0.5.

statement.p: For what values of x does it appear that \sin(x) \approx T_3(x)?

solution.p: The polynomial T_3(x) = x - \dfrac{x^3}{3!}. It appears that \sin(x) \approx T_3(x) for roughly |x| \lesssim 1.5.

statement.p: For what values of x does it appear that \sin(x) \approx T_5(x)?

solution.p: The polynomial T_5(x) = x - \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \dfrac{x^5}{5!}. It appears that \sin(x) \approx T_5(x) for roughly |x| \lesssim 2.5.

statement.p: What overall trend do you observe? How good is the approximation generated by T_{19}(x)?

solution.p: Each additional term in the polynomial extends the range of x-values over which the approximation is accurate. T_{19}(x) provides an excellent approximation to \sin(x) over a very large interval, essentially indistinguishable from \sin(x) on most standard viewing windows.

statement.p: In a new Desmos worksheet, plot the function y = \cos(x) along with the following functions: P_2(x) = 1 - \frac{x^2}{2!} and P_4(x) = 1 - \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!}. Based on the patterns with the coefficients in the polynomials approximating \sin(x) and the polynomials P_2 and P_4 here, conjecture formulas for P_6, P_8, and P_{18} and plot them. How well can we approximate y = \cos(x) using polynomials?

solution.p:
Following the pattern of alternating signs and even powers, the next polynomials are
P_6(x) &= 1 - \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} - \frac{x^6}{6!}, P_8(x) &= 1 - \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} - \frac{x^6}{6!} + \frac{x^8}{8!}, P_{18}(x) &= \sum_{j=0}^{9} \frac{(-1)^j x^{2j}}{(2j)!}.
Polynomial approximations can approximate y = \cos(x) just as well as y = \sin(x); each added term extends the range of accuracy.

T_1(x)=x is good near x=0; each higher-degree polynomial extends the interval of accuracy. T_{19} approximates \sin(x) extremely well. The cosine analogues follow the pattern P_{2k}(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{(-1)^j x^{2j}}{(2j)!}.
